title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
problem_statement: Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.
intuition: Keep track of the smallest possible tail value for increasing subsequences of each length, and extend or replace tails using binary search.

approaches.name: Recursive Brute Force
approaches.description: Try every subsequence by deciding whether to include or skip each number while maintaining the last chosen value.
approaches.why_not: Exponential due to exploring all subsets of indices. Does not scale beyond n~30.

approaches.name: DP with O(n^2)
approaches.description: Use dp[i] to store the LIS ending at index i by scanning previous indices for smaller values.
approaches.why_not: Correct for n up to 2500, but O(n log n) is faster for larger arrays.

approaches.name: DP with Binary Search (Patience Sorting)
approaches.description: Maintain a tails array where tails[len] is the smallest ending value of any increasing subsequence of length len+1. Use binary search to place each number.
approaches.why_not: This is the best known time complexity for LIS length.

interview_qa.a: The smallest ending value for an increasing subsequence of length k+1.

interview_qa.q: Why replace a tail value instead of append?
interview_qa.a: Replacing with a smaller value gives more chance to extend the subsequence later.
For n around 2500, O(n^2) is often acceptable; but O(n log n) is preferred when performance matters.
To find the first tails position that is >= current number, so we can update the smallest valid tail efficiently.
